critique.summary: The article introduces Gitana 18, a 32-meter flying Ultim trimaran launched as a technological evolution in offshore racing multihulls. It highlights the boat's advanced design features such as adjustable foils, cavitation-resistant rudders, integrated monocoque structure, dynamic rigging, and sophisticated mechatronic systems, positioning it as both a racing vessel and a technological demonstrator.
mainPoints: Gitana 18 features variable-geometry Y-shaped foils inspired by America's Cup AC75s for controlled flight and high-speed stability.
The trimaran uses three U-shaped rudders and a large central daggerboard designed to limit cavitation and maintain stable flight in heavy seas.
The monocoque structure integrates the cockpit and deckhouse to increase rigidity and reduce parasitic movements.
Movable spreaders allow dynamic adjustment of rigging tension to optimize mainsail power during sailing.
The boat is equipped with advanced mechatronic systems, including 44 hydraulic cylinders and an electronic control unit for flight management.
Gitana 18 serves as a technological demonstrator developed over 36 months with over 200 experts, aiming to achieve stable, fast, and controlled offshore flight.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a detailed description of Gitana 18's design and technological innovations consistent with known developments in the Ultim trimaran class and offshore racing technology. The references to America's Cup AC75 foils and advanced materials align with current industry trends. However, the article lacks citations from independent sources or multiple perspectives, relying solely on information likely provided by the Gitana Team or affiliated parties. The claim of averaging 40 knots in three-meter waves is based on simulation projections rather than verified sea trials. Overall, the technical claims appear plausible and consistent with expert knowledge but would benefit from external verification.
considerations: The article does not provide independent verification or third-party expert opinions on the performance claims.
There is no mention of potential challenges or limitations related to the complexity and maintenance demands of the advanced mechatronic systems.
The impact of weather conditions beyond the stated simulations on real-world performance is not discussed.
The article does not address the cost implications or accessibility of such advanced technology for broader sailing communities.
Environmental considerations or sustainability aspects of the materials and technologies used are not mentioned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the impressive technical advancements and design of the Gitana 18 flying trimaran, with commenters reflecting on its speed, engineering, and the evolving nature of sailing from traditional to high-tech. Some express awe and curiosity about the economics and human effort behind such projects, while others debate terminology and the cultural shift in sailing.

comments.author: jacquesm
comments.text:
Amazing. I saw the introduction of 'high tech fabrics' into sails up close when I was working with/for TD Sails in the Netherlands. The owner was - besides a very nice guy - into materials technology and math and the combination was quite interesting. He was also a visionary, spending a lot of money on CAD when everybody else was still laying out sails by hand and attempting to automate the fabric cutting stage. This was just when water jets were becoming feasible but I don't think he ever managed to get their cutting table to work.

Theo Dokman more or less predicted that the sailing industry and the aircraft industry would converge in terms of high tech while the customers were still asking for 1880's style 'brown' cloth sails for the traditional Dutch fleet.

He would have been super happy to see this, this (and some predecessors) validates pretty much everything he talked about. I'm absolutely amazed at the specs of this vessel, if you take into consideration the length of the hull and the speeds it can attain and in what kind of sea states it is able to do so. The difference between 'theoretically possible' and 'let's build it' here is so large that I wonder what the total bill for putting this out there was.

Note that it hasn't gone hydroplaning yet (apparently the surfaces are not yet fitted), but they're slowly working up to it.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZjiGtwd8q4Q

around ~1 minute the interesting bits start.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, knowledgeable insights about sail technology and industry evolution, making it the most accurate and informative in the discussion.

comments.author: Peteragain
comments.text: There is indeed something beautiful about traditional boats but this is a different kind of beauty. And 40 knots in 3 metre waves? Wow! Like F1 cars don't drive like road cars the automated control means this is not a boat but something else wonderous.
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced perspective on the aesthetic and technical differences between traditional and modern boats, accurately noting the advanced control systems.

comments.author: world2vec
comments.text: At some point it's more a "weird shaped airplane flies close to the water" than a sailing boat. It sure does look super cool tho.
comments.analysis: Makes a valid analogy comparing the trimaran to an airplane, reflecting the hybrid nature of the technology involved.

comments.author: vessenes
comments.text: They claim simulations estimate ability to stay aloft at 30 knots in 3 meter waves. WOWWWW. My stomach churns for multiple reasons imagining that, and the pitchpole flip at the end. Urgh.
comments.analysis: Expresses a visceral reaction to the technical claims and potential risks, which is understandable though less analytical.

comments.author: titanomachy
comments.text:
> 50k hours of design, 200k hours and 100 people to build

Wow, this is an enormous amount of wealth and human effort spent on a sport that I'm barely aware of. I'm curious about the economics of it; is there enough of a spectator base to make this profitable, or is it mostly just a few ultra-wealthy patrons?
comments.analysis: Raises reasonable questions about the economics and scale of investment in such projects, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: zingar
comments.text: I love me some hulls out of the water but I have a quibble with the term “flying” when there’s still something in the water and taking everything out of the water is dangerous, even of it is only a tiny fraction of the boat… have hydrofoils always been spoken of as flying or is that more recent hype?
comments.analysis: Critiques the terminology used, providing a nuanced view on the use of 'flying' in sailing contexts.

comments.author: telesilla
comments.text:
I grew up watching racing thanks to my grandad's interest but all with all the tech involved in these high end machines it's like watching jetplanes or something mechanical, maybe it's because I can't connect anymore to what's happening on a human level. I love the thrill of a fast boat but it's lost me on the accessibility that I remember from the 80s and 90s. I remember how crews and captains would be celebrities, now it seems it's a tech game?

Edit: it's a beautiful machine, regardless. https://www.youtube.com/shorts/Ruh3hASFyGw
comments.analysis: Reflects personal nostalgia and cultural observations about the shift from human-centered to technology-centered sailing.

comments.author: normie3000
comments.text: Does the tech advancing yacht racing transfer to industrial or social uses?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about technology transfer, though it lacks detail or evidence.

comments.author: Sparkyte
comments.text: Thought it said Gintama for a moment. The boat looks pretty neat though.
comments.analysis: A lighthearted, off-topic comment that adds little substantive value to the discussion.
